skill.name: error-handler-advisor
skill.description: Reviews Rust error handling patterns and suggests safer, more idiomatic use of Result, Option, thiserror, anyhow, and contextual propagation.
skill.summary: Advises on robust Rust error handling and panic reduction.
skill.author: EmilLindfors

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found they are false positives in Markdown front matter, inline code, and Rust example blocks. No runnable scripts, network calls, secret access, prompt injection, or malicious intent were found in the reviewed skill file.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Misread as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The external command detections are Markdown inline code and fenced Rust examples, not Ruby or shell backtick execution. The skill front matter only allows Read, Grep, and Glob, and no executable command runner is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are visible Markdown and Rust snippets used as teaching examples. No evidence found of shell execution syntax being used as executable skill behavior.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography detections do not correspond to hash, cipher, or signature code. The cited lines contain the skill description and prose about error handling, type safety, and application error libraries.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact cited lines contain natural-language Rust error handling guidance. No evidence found of cryptographic APIs, algorithms, or security-sensitive hashing.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance Pattern
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance detections are Rust thiserror message examples and do not inspect the host, environment, filesystem, processes, or network. They are static code examples for application error variants.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are error message strings or enum variants in Rust examples. No evidence found of reconnaissance commands or host data collection.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Obfuscation Heuristic on Markdown Structure
low_findings.description: The obfuscation heuristic appears to trigger on Markdown syntax and front matter. The file is readable prose with Rust examples and contains no encoded payload, hidden script, or prompt injection attempt.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line is the opening YAML front matter delimiter. A targeted prompt-injection keyword search found no suspicious override or authority claims.

content.value_statement: Rust projects can hide panics, weak error types, and missing context in routine code. This skill reviews error handling patterns and suggests idiomatic Result-based improvements.
actual_capabilities: Identifies unwrap, expect, and panic usage that can crash production Rust code.
Suggests Result and Option propagation patterns using the question mark operator.
Recommends thiserror for typed library errors and anyhow for application errors.
Adds guidance for preserving source errors with From conversions.
Encourages contextual error messages that improve debugging.
Reviews tests for expected error cases and panic paths.
limitations: It provides guidance and examples, not automated code rewriting.
It focuses on Rust error handling and is not a general Rust linter.
It cannot prove runtime behavior without project tests and execution context.
It does not replace security review for sensitive failure paths.

use_cases.title: Reduce Panic Risk in Services
use_cases.description: Review Rust service code for unwrap, expect, and panic calls before deployment.

use_cases.title: Design Library Error Types
use_cases.description: Create clear public error enums that preserve source errors and support caller matching.

use_cases.title: Improve Debugging Context
use_cases.description: Add operation-specific context to propagated errors so incidents are easier to diagnose.

prompt_templates.title: Find Basic Panic Points
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a small function.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Rust function for unwrap, expect, and panic usage. Explain each risk and suggest a Result-based alternative.

prompt_templates.title: Improve Error Propagation
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate cleanup of repetitive Result handling.
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this Rust code for verbose or lossy error propagation. Suggest where the question mark operator and context would improve it.

prompt_templates.title: Choose Error Libraries
prompt_templates.scenario: Project design decision for library or application code.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Rust module and recommend whether thiserror, anyhow, or both fit the error handling needs. Explain the tradeoffs.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Failure Semantics
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review of production error behavior.
prompt_templates.prompt: Perform a detailed error-handling audit of this Rust component. Prioritize panic risk, lost source errors, weak public error types, and missing tests.

output_examples.input: A database lookup uses unwrap after calling a fallible function.
output_examples.output: The skill flags a panic risk, recommends returning a Result, and explains how propagation lets callers handle missing data.

output_examples.input: A library returns string errors from validation functions.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends a typed error enum, explains why callers can match variants, and suggests preserving useful validation details.

output_examples.input: An application chains several fallible startup operations.
output_examples.output: The skill suggests contextual messages for each operation so logs show which startup step failed.

best_practices: Use the skill on real functions or modules so recommendations match the surrounding error model.
State whether the code is a library, CLI, service, or prototype before requesting advice.
Ask for test coverage suggestions when changing public error behavior.
anti_patterns: Do not ask it to remove every panic without explaining whether any invariant failures are intentional.
Do not apply anyhow to public library APIs without considering typed error contracts.
Do not accept broad rewrites without checking compatibility with existing callers.

faq.question: Does this skill execute code?
faq.answer: No. It is a review and guidance skill that reads code and suggests Rust error handling improvements.

faq.question: Which languages does it support?
faq.answer: It is focused on Rust and common Rust error handling crates such as thiserror and anyhow.

faq.question: Can it replace Clippy or compiler checks?
faq.answer: No. It complements tooling by explaining design tradeoffs and reviewing error semantics.

faq.question: Is it better for libraries or applications?
faq.answer: It supports both. It recommends typed errors for libraries and flexible context for applications.

faq.question: Can it help with tests?
faq.answer: Yes. It can suggest tests for expected error variants, propagation paths, and panic removal.

faq.question: Does it make security decisions?
faq.answer: No. It can reduce crash risk and improve diagnostics, but it is not a full security auditor.
